ss_final_code:
Algorithm 1: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)
1Initialize policy parameters θ = random_init()
2Initialize value function parameters φ = random_init()
3Set hyperparameters: ε = 0.2, λ = 0.95, γ = 0.99
4Set learning rates: α_π = 3e-4, α_v = 1e-3
5Set batch size B = 64, epochs K = 10
6for iteration = 1 to max_iterations:
7 trajectories = []
8 for t = 1 to T: # Collect trajectories
9 action = sample_action(π(·|state; θ))
10 next_state, reward = environment.step(action)
11 store (state, action, reward, next_state)
12 state = next_state
13 for each trajectory: # Compute advantages
14 values = V(states; φ)
15 deltas = rewards + γ * values[1:] - values[:-1]
16 advantages = compute_gae(deltas, λ, γ)
17 returns = advantages + values[:-1]
18 normalize(advantages)
19 for epoch = 1 to K: # Optimization epochs
20 shuffle_data(states, actions, advantages, returns)
21 for batch in minibatches(B):
22 old_log_probs = log π(actions|states; θ_old)
23 new_log_probs = log π(actions|states; θ)
24 ratio = exp(new_log_probs - old_log_probs)
25 surr1 = ratio * advantages
26 surr2 = clip(ratio, 1-ε, 1+ε) * advantages
27 L_clip = -mean(min(surr1, surr2))
28 θ = θ - α_π * ∇_θ L_clip
29 φ = φ - α_v * ∇_φ MSE(V(states; φ), returns)
30return θ, φ
Rollout Phase
Collect trajectories using current policy π(·|·; θ)
Advantage Estimation
Compute GAE advantages for policy gradient
Policy Update
Multiple epochs of minibatch optimization
Clipping prevents large updates
Ratio is clipped to [1-ε, 1+ε]

Initialize policy parameters θ
Initialize value function parameters φ
Set hyperparameters: ε, α, γ, λ
Set batch size N, epochs K
Set trajectory length T
for iteration = 1 to M do
for actor = 1 to N do
Run policy πθ in environment for T timesteps
Collect states st, actions at, rewards rt
Compute log probabilities log πθ(at|st)
Compute state values Vφ(st)
end for
// Compute advantages using GAE
for t = T-1 downto 0 do
δt = rt + γVφ(st+1) - Vφ(st)
At = δt + γλAt+1
Rt = At + Vφ(st)
end for
for epoch = 1 to K do
Sample random minibatch from collected data
for each minibatch do
Compute importance ratio:
rt(θ) = πθ(at|st) / πθ_old(at|st)
Compute surrogate losses:
LCLIP = min(rt(θ)At, clip(rt(θ), 1-ε, 1+ε)At)
Update θ by ascending gradient of LCLIP
end for
Update φ by regression on (st, Rt) pairs
end for

ss_final_code:
Code Walkthrough: Attention Mechanism

1 import torch
2 import torch.nn as nn
3
4 class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
5 def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads, dropout=0.1, bias=True):
6 super().__init__()
7 self.embed_dim = embed_dim
8 self.num_heads = num_heads
9 self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads
10 self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
11
12 def forward(self, query, key, value):
13 batch_size, seq_len, _ = query.shape
14 Q = query.view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim)
15 scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.head_dim)
16 attention_weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
17 output = torch.matmul(self.dropout(attention_weights), V)
18 return output.view(batch_size, seq_len, self.embed_dim)
1. Class inherits from nn.Module for PyTorch integration
◀ 2. head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads splits dimensions across attention heads
3. Forward pass accepts Q/K/V tensors of shape (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)
◀ 4. Reshape splits embedding into multiple heads for parallel attention
5. Attention weights computed via scaled dot-product of queries and keys
◀ 6. Softmax normalizes attention scores to create probability distribution
7. Output projection combines all attention heads back to original dimension
rv_final_code:
Code Walkthrough: Attention Mechanism
1 import torch.nn as nn
2 import torch.nn.functional as F
3
4 class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
5 def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads, dropout, bias):
6 super().__init__()
7 self.embed_dim = embed_dim
8 self.num_heads = num_heads
9 self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads
10 self.qkv_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, 3 * embed_dim)
11 self.out_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
12 def forward(self, query, key, value):
13 qkv = self.qkv_proj(query).reshape(-1, 3, self.num_heads)
14 q, k, v = qkv.unbind(1)
15 attn = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1))
16 attn = F.softmax(attn, dim=-1)
17 output = torch.matmul(attn, v)
18 return self.out_proj(output)
  • Class inherits from nn.Module for PyTorch integration
  • head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads splits dimensions across attention heads
  • Forward pass accepts Q/K/V tensors as input parameters
  • Reshape operation splits embedding into multiple attention heads
  • Attention weights computed via scaled dot-product of Q and K matrices
  • Softmax normalizes attention scores to create probability distribution
  • Output projection combines all attention heads back into single representation

ss_final_code:
Quarterly Business Review

Financial Performance and Strategic Outlook for Q4 2025

Our revenue growth trajectory has exceeded expectations this quarter, with a remarkable 24% increase compared to the same period last year¹. The strategic initiatives implemented in early Q3 have begun to yield substantial returns across all business segments. Our compound annual growth rate calculation using CAGR = (Vfinal / Vinitial)(1/n) - 1 demonstrates consistent upward momentum.

Market expansion efforts have successfully penetrated three new geographical regions, establishing strong partnerships and distribution networks². The diversification strategy has reduced our dependency on traditional markets while opening new revenue streams. Customer acquisition costs have decreased by 18% while retention rates have improved significantly³.

Region Revenue Growth
North America $2.4M +15%
Europe $1.8M +22%
Asia Pacific $1.2M +38%
Latin America $0.7M +45%
1 Based on audited financial statements and quarterly reporting standards as of December 31, 2024
2 Market expansion includes strategic partnerships in Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe, and South America
3 Customer metrics calculated using rolling 12-month averages and cohort analysis methodologies

ss_final_code:
Deep Learning for Natural Language Understanding: A Survey
The transformer architecture[Vaswani et al., 2017] revolutionized NLP by introducing self-attention mechanisms. Subsequent work on BERT[Devlin et al., 2019] demonstrated the power of bidirectional pre-training, achieving state-of-the-art results on 11 benchmarks. These foundational models established new paradigms for natural language understanding tasks. The impact of these architectures continues to influence modern NLP research directions.
Large language models such as GPT-3[Brown et al., 2020] showed that scaling model size leads to emergent capabilities. The instruction-following paradigm[Ouyang et al., 2022] further aligned these models with human preferences through RLHF. This approach demonstrated significant improvements in model helpfulness and safety. The combination of scale and alignment has become a cornerstone of modern language model development.
Recent advances in efficient attention[Dao et al., 2022] have reduced the quadratic complexity bottleneck. Constitutional AI methods[Bai et al., 2022] provide new frameworks for safe and helpful AI systems. These developments address both computational efficiency and ethical considerations in AI deployment.
References
Bai, Y., Jones, A., Ndousse, K., Askell, A., Chen, A., DasSarma, N., ... & Kaplan, J. (2022). Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI feedback. arXiv preprint arXiv:2212.08073.
Brown, T., Mann, B., Ryder, N., Subbiah, M., Kaplan, J. D., Dhariwal, P., ... & Amodei, D. (2020). Language models are few-shot learners. Advances in neural information processing systems, 33, 1877-1901.
Dao, T., Fu, D. Y., Ermon, S., Rudra, A., & Ré, C. (2022). FlashAttention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with IO-awareness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 16344-16359.
Devlin, J., Chang, M. W., Lee, K., & Toutanova, K. (2019). BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding. Proceedings of the 2019 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics, 4171-4186.
Ouyang, L., Wu, J., Jiang, X., Almeida, D., Wainwright, C., Mishkin, P., ... & Lowe, R. (2022). Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 27730-27744.
Vaswani, A., Shazeer, N., Parmar, N., Uszkoreit, J., Jones, L., Gomez, A. N., ... & Polosukhin, I. (2017). Attention is all you need. Advances in neural information processing systems, 30, 5998-6008.
rv_final_code:
Deep Learning for Natural Language Understanding: A Survey
The transformer architecture [Vaswani et al., 2017] revolutionized NLP by introducing self-attention mechanisms. Subsequent work on BERT [Devlin et al., 2019] demonstrated the power of bidirectional pre-training, achieving state-of-the-art results on 11 benchmarks. This breakthrough established the foundation for modern language understanding systems. The bidirectional nature of BERT enabled richer contextual representations compared to previous unidirectional approaches.
Large language models such as GPT-3 [Brown et al., 2020] showed that scaling model size leads to emergent capabilities. The instruction-following paradigm [Ouyang et al., 2022] further aligned these models with human preferences through RLHF. These developments marked a shift from task-specific fine-tuning to general-purpose language understanding. The emergence of few-shot learning capabilities demonstrated the potential for more flexible AI systems.
Recent advances in efficient attention [Dao et al., 2022] have reduced the quadratic complexity bottleneck. Constitutional AI methods [Bai et al., 2022] provide new frameworks for safe and helpful AI systems. These innovations address both computational efficiency and alignment challenges in modern NLP.
References
Bai, Y., Jones, A., Ndousse, K., Askell, A., Chen, A., DasSarma, N., ... & Kaplan, J. (2022). Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI feedback. arXiv preprint arXiv:2212.08073.
Brown, T., Mann, B., Ryder, N., Subbiah, M., Kaplan, J. D., Dhariwal, P., ... & Amodei, D. (2020). Language models are few-shot learners. Advances in neural information processing systems, 33, 1877-1901.
Dao, T., Fu, D., Ermon, S., Rudra, A., & Ré, C. (2022). FlashAttention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with IO-awareness. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 16344-16359.
Devlin, J., Chang, M. W., Lee, K., & Toutanova, K. (2019). BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding. Proceedings of NAACL-HLT, 4171-4186.
Ouyang, L., Wu, J., Jiang, X., Almeida, D., Wainwright, C., Mishkin, P., ... & Lowe, R. (2022). Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35, 27730-27744.
Vaswani, A., Shazeer, N., Parmar, N., Uszkoreit, J., Jones, L., Gomez, A. N., ... & Polosukhin, I. (2017). Attention is all you need. Advances in neural information processing systems, 30.

ss_final_code:
Computer Vision in Autonomous Driving
LiDAR Point Cloud Visualization

Object detection in autonomous vehicles relies heavily on advanced computer vision algorithms that process multiple sensor inputs simultaneously. Modern systems utilize convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformer architectures to identify pedestrians, vehicles, traffic signs, and road markings with remarkable accuracy. These algorithms must operate under diverse lighting conditions, weather scenarios, and complex urban environments while maintaining real-time performance requirements.

Sensor fusion represents a critical component where data from cameras, LiDAR, radar, and ultrasonic sensors are combined to create a comprehensive understanding of the vehicle's surroundings. Each sensor modality provides unique advantages: cameras offer rich color and texture information, LiDAR provides precise depth measurements, while radar excels in adverse weather conditions. The integration of these heterogeneous data streams requires sophisticated calibration procedures and temporal synchronization mechanisms.

Real-time processing constraints demand optimized inference pipelines that can process high-resolution sensor data at frequencies exceeding 30 Hz. Edge computing solutions, including specialized AI accelerators and GPU clusters, enable the deployment of complex deep learning models within the stringent latency requirements of automotive applications. Advanced techniques such as model quantization, pruning, and knowledge distillation are employed to reduce computational overhead while preserving detection accuracy.

Safety considerations in autonomous driving systems require redundant perception pathways and fail-safe mechanisms to handle sensor failures or unexpected scenarios. The development of safety-critical software follows rigorous standards such as ISO 26262, which mandates extensive testing, validation, and verification procedures. Emergency braking systems and human-machine interfaces provide additional layers of protection when the primary perception system encounters challenging or ambiguous situations.

Regulatory requirements and standardization efforts continue to evolve as autonomous vehicle technology advances toward commercial deployment. Government agencies worldwide are establishing frameworks for testing, validation, and certification of autonomous systems, while industry consortiums work to develop common standards for sensor interfaces, communication protocols, and safety assessment methodologies. These regulatory landscapes directly influence the design and implementation of computer vision systems in production vehicles.

Figure 1: 3D LiDAR point cloud showing detected objects with bounding boxes. Data collected from Waymo Open Dataset, Scene ID: 10203656353524179475.
© 2025 Autonomous Systems Lab | Stanford University
rv_final_code:
Computer Vision in Autonomous Driving

LiDAR Point Cloud Visualization

Object detection in autonomous vehicles relies heavily on sophisticated computer vision algorithms that process multiple data streams in real-time. Advanced neural networks, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformer architectures, enable vehicles to identify and classify pedestrians, vehicles, traffic signs, and road infrastructure with remarkable accuracy. These systems must operate reliably under varying lighting conditions, weather patterns, and environmental complexities.

Sensor fusion techniques combine data from cameras, LiDAR, radar, and ultrasonic sensors to create comprehensive environmental understanding. Each sensor modality provides unique advantages: cameras offer rich visual detail and color information, LiDAR provides precise distance measurements and 3D spatial mapping, while radar excels in adverse weather conditions. Machine learning algorithms integrate these heterogeneous data sources to generate robust perception models that exceed the capabilities of individual sensors.

Real-time processing requirements demand optimized computational architectures capable of handling massive data throughput with minimal latency. Edge computing solutions, specialized AI accelerators, and distributed processing frameworks enable vehicles to make critical decisions within milliseconds. Advanced optimization techniques, including model quantization, pruning, and knowledge distillation, reduce computational overhead while maintaining detection accuracy essential for safe autonomous operation in dynamic traffic environments.

Safety considerations in autonomous driving systems require redundant perception pathways and fail-safe mechanisms to handle edge cases and sensor failures. Comprehensive testing protocols include millions of simulation scenarios, closed-course validation, and extensive real-world testing across diverse geographical regions and traffic conditions. Emergency braking systems, human oversight capabilities, and graceful degradation protocols ensure passenger and pedestrian safety even when primary perception systems encounter unexpected situations or technical malfunctions.

Regulatory requirements and industry standards continue evolving as autonomous vehicle technology matures and deployment scales increase. International organizations, government agencies, and automotive manufacturers collaborate to establish comprehensive safety protocols, testing methodologies, and certification processes. These frameworks address data privacy concerns, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, ethical decision-making algorithms, and liability considerations while promoting innovation and public acceptance of autonomous transportation technologies.

Figure 1: 3D LiDAR point cloud showing detected objects with bounding boxes. Data collected from Waymo Open Dataset, Scene ID: 10203656353524179475.
© 2025 Autonomous Systems Lab | Stanford University
